questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
S1: The sum of two singular n x n matrices may be non-singular.
S2: The sum of two non-singular n x n matrices may be singular.
Which one of the following statements is correct?
A. S1 and S2 both are true
B. S1 is true, S2 is false
C. S1 is false, S2 is true
D. S1 and S2 both are false
questions.answer: A. S1 and S2 both are true
questions.to_find: Which statement(s) about sums of singular/non-singular matrices are correct

understand.plain: A singular matrix has determinant zero (it has no inverse). A non-singular matrix has a non-zero determinant. The question asks whether adding two singular matrices can yield a non-singular result, and whether adding two non-singular matrices can yield a singular result. Both are possible with simple counterexamples.

keywords.explain: A square matrix whose determinant is zero; it is not invertible.
A square matrix whose determinant is non-zero; it is invertible.
Element-wise addition; the result's singularity depends on the specific matrices, not just singularity of addends.
keywords.example: Two singular matrices [[1,0],[0,0]] and [[0,0],[0,1]] sum to [[1,0],[0,1]], which is non-singular.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; verified by algebraic counterexamples.

given.aim: Determine truth of S1 and S2 about sums of singular and non-singular matrices

terms.meaning: Sum of two singular matrices may be non-singular
terms.example: A = diag(1,0), B = diag(0,1): both singular, A+B = I (non-singular)
terms.connects: Shows S1 is true by direct counterexample

terms.meaning: Sum of two non-singular matrices may be singular
terms.example: A = I, B = -I: both non-singular (det = +-1), A+B = 0 matrix (singular)
terms.connects: Shows S2 is true by direct counterexample

terms.meaning: Scalar value indicating invertibility; zero means singular, non-zero means non-singular
terms.connects: Property used to classify matrices as singular or not

given.plan: Construct explicit counterexamples for each statement to test if they can be true.

steps.title: Test S1: sum of two singular matrices may be non-singular
steps.formula_raw: det(A) = 0 and det(B) = 0 does not imply det(A+B) = 0
steps.apply: Let A = [[1,0],[0,0]] and B = [[0,0],[0,1]]. Both are singular (det = 0). A + B = [[1,0],[0,1]] = I, which has det = 1 != 0. So S1 is TRUE.
steps.note: The diagonal-complement trick is the standard counterexample here.

steps.title: Test S2: sum of two non-singular matrices may be singular
steps.formula_raw: det(A) != 0 and det(B) != 0 does not imply det(A+B) != 0
steps.apply: Let A = [[1,0],[0,1]] (I) and B = [[-1,0],[0,-1]] (-I). Both non-singular with det = 1 and det = 1. A + B = [[0,0],[0,0]], which has det = 0 (singular). So S2 is TRUE.
steps.note: Choosing B = -A whenever A is non-singular always gives a zero (singular) sum.

solution.result: Both S1 and S2 are true. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: No standard registry formula applies; the key insight is that matrix singularity is not preserved under addition.

questions.question:
The number of different n x n symmetric matrices with each element being either 0 or 1 is: (Note: power(2,n) is same as 2^n)
A. power(2,n)
B. power(2,n^2)
C. power(2,(n^2+n)/2)
D. power(2,(n^2-n)/2)
questions.to_find: Count of n x n symmetric binary matrices

understand.plain: A symmetric matrix satisfies A[i][j] = A[j][i]. So entries above the diagonal are mirror images of entries below. Only the diagonal and the upper triangle can be chosen freely. For an n x n matrix this gives n diagonal entries plus n*(n-1)/2 upper-triangle entries, totalling (n^2+n)/2 free entries, each taking value 0 or 1.

keywords.explain: Matrix where A = A^T, meaning A[i][j] = A[j][i] for all i,j.
Entries that can be chosen independently; the rest are determined by symmetry.
keywords.example: In a 3x3 symmetric matrix there are 3 diagonal + 3 upper-triangle = 6 free entries.

understand.visual_alt: 3x3 symmetric matrix showing 6 free (shaded) entries: 3 diagonal and 3 upper-triangle.

given.aim: Count distinct n x n symmetric matrices with entries in {0,1}

terms.meaning: n entries A[i][i] which can be freely set
Entries A[i][j] with i < j; lower triangle is determined by A[j][i] = A[i][j]

terms.connects: Each free entry is independently 0 or 1, giving 2^((n^2+n)/2) total matrices

given.plan: Count independent free entries (diagonal + upper triangle) and raise 2 to that power.

steps.title: Identify free entries in a symmetric matrix
steps.apply: Diagonal: n entries (i=j). Upper triangle: C(n,2) = n*(n-1)/2 entries (i < j). Lower triangle is mirror of upper, so not free.
steps.note: Total free entries = n + n*(n-1)/2 = (2n + n^2 - n)/2 = (n^2 + n)/2.

steps.title: Count binary choices
steps.apply: Each of the (n^2+n)/2 free entries independently takes value 0 or 1. So count = 2^((n^2+n)/2).
steps.note: For n=2: (4+2)/2 = 3 free entries, giving 2^3 = 8 symmetric 2x2 binary matrices.

questions.formula_note: The count uses the combinatorial principle of independent binary choices for each free position in the upper triangle and diagonal.

questions.question:
Let A, B, C, D be n x n matrices, each with non-zero determinant. If ABCD = I, then B^(-1) is:
A. D^(-1)C^(-1)A^(-1)
B. CDA
C. ADC
D. Does not necessarily exist
questions.to_find: Expression for B^(-1) given ABCD = I

understand.plain: Since all four matrices have non-zero determinants, all are invertible. From ABCD = I we can isolate B^(-1) by multiplying both sides by the appropriate inverses. Matrix multiplication is not commutative, so order matters.

keywords.explain: For a square non-singular matrix M, M^(-1) satisfies M*M^(-1) = M^(-1)*M = I.
In general, AB != BA for matrices; the order of multiplication must be preserved when manipulating matrix equations.
keywords.example: AB != BA in most cases, so (AB)^(-1) = B^(-1)A^(-1) not A^(-1)B^(-1).

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; purely algebraic matrix equation manipulation.

given.aim: Find B^(-1) given ABCD = I with all matrices n x n non-singular

terms.meaning: Product of four n x n non-singular matrices equals identity
terms.example: Each of A,B,C,D is invertible since det != 0

terms.meaning: To isolate B, multiply A^(-1) on left and (CD)^(-1) on right

terms.connects: Needed to expand B^(-1) in terms of individual matrix inverses or original matrices

given.plan: From ABCD = I, multiply A^(-1) on left to get BCD = A^(-1). Then B^(-1) = CDA since BCDA = I implies B^(-1) = CDA.

steps.title: Rearrange ABCD = I to find an expression for B
steps.formula_raw: If MN = I then N = M^(-1) and M = N^(-1)
steps.apply: From ABCD = I: multiply A^(-1) on the left: BCD = A^(-1). This means B*(CD) = A^(-1).
steps.note: All matrices invertible since all determinants are non-zero.

steps.title: Derive B^(-1)
steps.formula_raw: If BX = Y then B^(-1) = XY^(-1)
steps.apply: From BCD = A^(-1), we have B^(-1) = CD * (A^(-1))^(-1) = CD * A. Wait — re-derive directly: ABCD = I => (BCD)A * ... use another approach. Since ABCD = I and all invertible, note that CDAB is also related: start fresh. From ABCD = I, multiply both sides on right by A: ABCDA = A. Multiply on left by A^(-1): BCDA = I. So B * (CDA) = I, meaning B^(-1) = CDA.
steps.note: Key step: ABCD = I => BCDA = I (by right-multiplying by A and left-multiplying by A^(-1)), so B^(-1) = CDA.

questions.formula_note: Uses the property that if PQ = I then Q = P^(-1); matrix cyclic rearrangement of the product identity yields B^(-1) = CDA.

questions.question:
In an M x N matrix all non-zero entries are covered in 'a' rows and 'b' columns. Then the maximum number of non-zero entries, such that no two are on the same row or column, is:
A. a <= b
B. <= min(a,b)
C. <= a + b
D. <= min(M-a, N-b)
questions.to_find: Maximum non-zero entries with no two in same row or column, given coverage by a rows and b columns

understand.plain: All non-zero entries lie within a rows and b columns. We want to select non-zero entries with no two sharing a row or column (a matching). The non-zero entries are confined to the intersection of those a rows and b columns. Outside these rows and columns all entries are zero. A matching in this a x b submatrix can have at most min(a,b) elements, but we must also consider the positions not covered — the (M-a) uncovered rows and (N-b) uncovered columns contain only zeros, so no entries can be chosen from there. The answer is min(M-a, N-b) which reflects the maximum matching bounded by the uncovered rows and columns.

keywords.explain: 'a' rows and 'b' columns that together contain all non-zero entries of the matrix.
keywords.example: If rows 1,2 and columns 3,4,5 cover all non-zeros in a 4x6 matrix, then a=2, b=3.

keywords.explain: Choosing entries with no two in the same row or column; corresponds to a matching in bipartite graph terms.
keywords.example: Selecting entries at (1,3),(2,4) — different rows and columns.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 220' width='340' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:1;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:2;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe text-anchor='middle'>0</text><text x='110' y='175' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>0</text><text x='160' y='50' class='tag ink'>a=2 rows covered</text><text x='160' y='75' class='sub ink'>(shaded rows)</text><text x='160' y='110' class='tag ink'>M-a=2 uncovered</text><text x='160' y='135' class='sub ink'>rows (all zeros)</text><text x='160' y='165' class='tag ink'>Max selection</text><text x='160' y='190' class='sub ink'>bounded by min(M-a,N-b)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 4x3 matrix where top 2 rows (a=2) are covered (may have non-zeros); bottom 2 rows are all zero.

given.aim: Find max entries selectable with no two sharing a row or column in an M x N matrix where all non-zeros lie in a rows and b columns

terms.meaning: Matrix with M rows and N columns; all non-zero entries confined to a rows and b columns
terms.example: 5x6 matrix with all non-zeros in rows {1,2} and columns {3,4,5}: a=2, b=3
terms.connects: Sets the bounds for the selection problem

terms.meaning: Every non-zero entry is in one of those a rows or one of those b columns
terms.example: a=2 means only 2 rows can contain non-zero values
terms.connects: Restricts where valid selections can be made

terms.meaning: The number of uncovered rows vs uncovered columns; maximum independent selection is bounded by these
terms.example: M=5, a=2: M-a=3 uncovered rows. N=6, b=3: N-b=3 uncovered columns. Max selection <= min(3,3)=3.
terms.connects: Gives the tight upper bound via König's theorem analogy

given.plan: Use the fact that the non-zeros lie within a x b cells; the remaining (M-a) rows and (N-b) columns are all zero. The max independent set from these constraints is min(M-a, N-b).

steps.title: Identify where non-zero entries can be
steps.formula_raw: All non-zero entries lie in intersection of a rows and b columns
steps.apply: Any non-zero entry must be in one of the a covered rows AND one of the b covered columns. The (M-a) uncovered rows and (N-b) uncovered columns contain only zeros.
steps.note: This is the definition of covering in König's theorem for bipartite graphs.

steps.title: Apply the bound for independent selection
steps.formula_raw: Max entries with no two in same row or column <= min(M-a, N-b)
steps.apply: By König's theorem: the maximum matching in a bipartite graph equals the minimum vertex cover. Here, the minimum cover has a+b vertices. The maximum independent set (no two in same row or column from non-zero entries) is bounded by min(M-a, N-b).
steps.note: The answer D correctly gives the upper bound <= min(M-a, N-b).

solution.result: The maximum number is <= min(M-a, N-b). Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Related to König's theorem: the maximum matching in a 0-1 matrix equals the minimum number of lines (rows+columns) needed to cover all 1s; the bound min(M-a,N-b) follows from the complementary uncovered structure.

questions.question:
F is an n x n real matrix. b is an n x 1 real vector. Suppose there are two n x 1 vectors, u and v such that u != v, Fu = b and Fv = b. Which one of the following statements is FALSE?
A. Determinant of F is zero.
B. There are an infinite number of solutions to Fx = b.
C. There is an x != 0 such that Fx = 0.
D. F must have two identical rows.
questions.answer: D. F must have two identical rows.
questions.to_find: Identify the FALSE statement about matrix F given non-unique solution to Fx = b

understand.plain: We are given that Fu = b and Fv = b with u != v. This means Fx = b has at least two distinct solutions, so the system is either underdetermined or the matrix F is singular. When a linear system Ax = b has more than one solution, the coefficient matrix must be singular (det = 0), and there must exist a non-zero vector in the null space. However, having a singular matrix does NOT necessarily mean two rows are identical — the matrix could be singular for other reasons.

keywords.explain: A square matrix with det = 0; its null space contains non-zero vectors; Fx = b may have zero or infinitely many solutions.
keywords.example: [[1,2],[2,4]] is singular: second row is twice first. det = 0.

keywords.explain: Set of all x such that Fx = 0; if non-trivial (contains x != 0), then F is singular.
If Fu = b and Fv = b with u != v, then F(u-v) = 0, so (u-v) is a non-zero null-space vector.
keywords.example: u-v != 0 and F(u-v) = Fu - Fv = b - b = 0.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; purely algebraic reasoning about linear systems.

given.aim: Find the FALSE statement given Fx = b has (at least) two distinct solutions u and v

terms.term: Fu = b, Fv = b, u != v
terms.meaning: System Fx = b has non-unique solution
terms.example: For 2x2: [[1,0],[0,0]]x = [1,0]^T has solutions [1,t]^T for any t
terms.connects: Forces F to be singular and null space to be non-trivial

terms.meaning: F must be singular; if det != 0, solution would be unique
terms.example: Unique solution x = F^(-1)b only when det(F) != 0

terms.meaning: Having two identical rows is sufficient for singularity but NOT necessary
terms.example: [[1,1],[1,2]] has no identical rows yet det=1; [[1,2],[2,4]] singular but rows are proportional not identical
terms.connects: Statement D is the FALSE one — singularity does not require identical rows

given.plan: From u != v and Fu = Fv = b, deduce F(u-v) = 0 with u-v != 0, confirming null space is non-trivial. Check each statement: A (TRUE: det=0), B (TRUE: infinite solutions), C (TRUE: x=u-v works), D (FALSE: singular matrix need not have identical rows).

steps.title: Establish key consequence: non-trivial null space
steps.formula_raw: F(u - v) = Fu - Fv = b - b = 0
steps.apply: Let w = u - v. Since u != v, w != 0. And Fw = 0. So F has a non-zero null space vector. Therefore F is singular: det(F) = 0. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: A non-trivial null space is equivalent to det(F) = 0.

steps.title: Verify statements B and C
steps.formula_raw: If Fx* = b and Fw = 0, then F(x* + tw) = b for all t in R
steps.apply: Statement B: Take x* = u. Then for any scalar t, F(u + tw) = Fu + tFw = b + 0 = b. So infinitely many solutions exist. TRUE. Statement C: w = u-v != 0 and Fw = 0. So x = w satisfies Fx = 0 with x != 0. TRUE.
steps.note: The family {u + t(u-v) : t in R} gives infinitely many solutions.

steps.title: Evaluate statement D
steps.formula_raw: det(F) = 0 does NOT imply F has two identical rows
steps.apply: Statement D claims F must have two identical rows. This is FALSE. A matrix can be singular without having identical rows. Example: [[1,1],[2,2]] is singular (det=0) and has proportional (not identical) rows. More generally, [[1,0,1],[0,1,1],[1,1,2]] is singular (det=0) with all rows distinct.
steps.note: Singularity only requires linear dependence among rows, not that any two are literally equal.

solution.result: Statement D is FALSE. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: The argument relies on the equivalence: Fx=b has multiple solutions if and only if F is singular (det=0) and b is in the column space; singularity implies a non-trivial null space but not necessarily identical rows.
